clinical_note: ACE inhibitors and ARBs reduce aldosterone and renal potassium excretion; rising potassium after initiation suggests drug-associated hyperkalemia.
A modest creatinine rise can follow RAAS blockade; a sustained rise may indicate haemodynamic renal impairment.
Declining eGFR after ACEi/ARB initiation may indicate reduced renal perfusion and warrants review.
Rising INR on warfarin increases bleeding risk; supratherapeutic INR requires dose review.
Metformin accumulates in renal impairment; falling eGFR raises lactic-acidosis concern and may require dose review or discontinuation.
Rising creatinine on metformin signals declining renal clearance and reduced drug elimination.
NSAIDs reduce renal prostaglandin-mediated perfusion; rising creatinine suggests possible NSAID-associated renal impairment.
Declining eGFR after NSAID exposure suggests reduced renal perfusion.
Loop and thiazide diuretics promote potassium loss; falling potassium may indicate diuretic-associated hypokalemia.
Thiazide diuretics can cause hyponatremia; a falling sodium trend warrants review.
Potassium-sparing diuretics retain potassium; rising potassium suggests drug-associated hyperkalemia.
Statins can elevate transaminases; a rising ALT trend may indicate hepatocellular stress.
SSRIs can cause SIADH-related hyponatremia; a falling sodium trend warrants review.
